notes.anchor: had I not put that word out of service for every season
notes.note: Cicero will not call Lucceius's letter "delightful" (iucundus): in his grief after Tullia's death he has retired that word from all use. The mournful pun answers Lucceius's reproach in Fam. 5.14 for shunning company.

notes.anchor: the Forum being common ground, neighbourhood is not required
notes.note: In the city the Forum Romanum is the shared meeting-place of all public men, so two friends need not live next door to see each other --- yet, Cicero notes, even there they never managed it. The Forum was the civic, judicial, and social heart of Rome.

notes.anchor: stripped of the adornments and consolations both of home and of the Forum
notes.note: Domesticis et forensibus ornamentis: Cicero's two ruined spheres --- his household (after Tullia's death) and his public life (after Pharsalus and Caesar's dominance). With both gone, only his books remain.

notes.anchor: for the times, for men, for the Forum, for the Senate-house
notes.note: The catalogue of what Cicero now loathes maps his withdrawal from public life under Caesar's dictatorship: the Forum (the courts) and the Curia (the Senate-house) had been the two arenas of his career. Both are now closed to free political action.
